## [00:00 - 03:00]

Bear with me on the computer, even though I have Megan as such a whiz on the computer and PowerPoint. One time last year, two years ago, Pauline and I were coming back from Europe on the Queen Elizabeth II. We don't like to fly and we always wanted to go on the Queen Elizabeth ship, so we were making the transatlantic crossing. And they had these great programs that they do on board because you're five days at sea across the North Atlantic. And one of the presentations they had was by the top flight engineer and pilot of the Concorde, a beautiful plane that is no longer flying. And he invited everyone on the ship interested in seeing his presentation to come to a PowerPoint demonstration of what the Concorde had flown and why it's the most technically difficult plane in the world to fly and what happened with the terrible accident. And it was something like an interesting presentation. All this world-class engineer and top pilot who trains other pilots on how to fly the most complex plane in the world could not get his PowerPoint to work. This may seem rather unusual for me to be recommending direct response design tips, but I've learned from the best, including people like Ed Elligott when he was doing design and people like Rob Davis, who is just a terrific designer and others. So I'm going to teach you what little I know about design and what little I know came from these very good designers. And if you think about it, the design of these new breakthrough packages when Magalox first came along, they created so much more success than any other packages style format before that, and it had nothing to do with copy. It was really the design of the packages. As Ogilvy had said, even in space, if you make it look like an editorial, you'll get automatically 500 percent higher readership. And in John Capil's case, he measured it, and the editorial-looking Ed pulled 81 percent higher in actual sales. That's not a copy function. That's design. But the copyrighter has to bear that in mind, even though you're not the designer and the marketer has to bear that in mind as you're writing the Ed, because some headlines may work better if they're destined to be in something that looks like an article. So you always have to be a little bit of a designer, at least, to take advantage of these editorial and value-laden formats and ads. OK. First one, make your advertising itself look valuable. We've covered that. Top of the line is in direct mail. There's a FedEx package if you can possibly figure out a product to sell that way or have one all the way down through the other options that we've spoken about. I used to say to Bob King, who is the general manager of Phillips for a long time, I have an ABA rule. It should look like anything but advertising, because advertising automatically brings in a, I'm probably going to reject this response, split second, I'm not interested in advertising right now. And you might want to test a Magalogue. Most of us copyrighters like to cram lots of bullets into our ador envelopes and copy.

And I migrated to something like that, which has a little less copy, a little more design. And I found that the more I went in that direction, the higher the response was going. ## [12:11 - 15:13]

Great grabber inside. I think the problem was that nobody really believed that America was going to have runaway inflation or if they did, they didn't want to think about it. But this was the actual note that was in the package. Or maybe it looked so valuable and was so valuable that somehow they disappeared from the post office and somebody, that might have been the reason, I don't know. Not totally out of the realm of possibility. Okay, design tip. Once you foot in the door, copy does drive the sale. As important as design is in getting your prospects to read your messages, don't think that design will normally sell in a superior way to copy. It's really, I've always felt that, as Ross Arif said, an ad agency is a transmission belt for a good piece of copy. That's kind of the attitude I always had working in the copy department. Where it starts with a copy and the designer can play a very important role in bringing out what the copywriter is trying to sell. But it usually doesn't work in reverse where the designer comes up with the visuals and then the copywriter writes around it. Once you're inside the package, copy is going to do your selling. But you should always work with a good direct response designer. On one test we did about a year ago for one of the products I worked on, we worked with a designer, very talented. She designs book covers and jackets and very, very creative. But she never really did a direct mail package before. And she did a very nice design. But right up until the last minute, we were catching things that were not right for the post office, not right for the printer. It was so many headaches that we had to correct them. We're not even sure we caught them all. And you really need a designer who knows direct response and specializes in direct response. The eye is the creature of habit. Don't fight it. For example, the eye is used to reading serif typefaces. And I know many of you know this. Almost all newspaper, I think all major newspapers are printed in serif type. Serif type is what I'm using in my, after number six. You see the letters have little squiggles on the end. And what they do is they sort of direct the eye all across the page. It's a very nice border that the eye subconsciously follows. And all major newspapers, most textbooks are created in this way with using serif type. San serif is straight up and down. No ligatures at the, like at the, look at the T. You'll see there's no footing at the bottom of the T in san serif type. So you want to always use serif type because that's what people are most used to reading. Tell you an interesting story. For one of the newsletters that we were writing, we were running an ad in the New York Times and we were right up against the deadline when the editor said, I got to change the recommendation. But I want to be into this Sunday Times. So let the, let the newspaper set a copy for the ad. So we sent the raw typewriter written copy, sent it to the New York Times and it just with instructions, run it on Sunday. But please set it with serif type. But somehow in the rush, the type setter got it wrong and he ran it in san serif type, all straight up and down letters. He did everything else exactly the way we told him. But then we screened in the New York Times on Monday when we got back to the office. Gee, we saw the ad yesterday, but you didn't set it the way we said. And they said, okay, well, we'll do it over. On us, take a free ad this Sunday and we'll now get it right. The following Sunday, they ran the ad as it was supposed to and there was an 86% increase

## [15:13 - 18:16]

in pull just from changing the type. Everything else was exactly the same, from san serif to serif. Now there was a weeks difference, so it wasn't exactly a split run test. Maybe the marketplace changed a little bit. I don't think so. It was, that's how powerful this principle can be. You want serif type only in body copy. It's fine, it's good there, but body copy, people get very tired of reading san serif for very long. If you put your headline above the illustration, you force readers to fight against reading gravity. For example, this is an ad that has a lot of problems for a lot of reasons. Well, here's a perfect example. If, let's say, you were judging this with the persuasion equation, you would say, well, what's the problem here that we're solving for our readers? There's no problem or need or want addressed. Just the proof, it just lacks in so many ways. This would be failing before we even got to the third question. But it fails on design also because visuals tend to attract the eye first. And so you want, usually in the space area, especially, you want your visual at the top of the page and your headline under it. That's a classic Ogilvy style design. So because here your eye sees the computer and then you have to fight against gravity. When we read something, we start at the top and we read left to right and top to bottom. We don't want to break that rule because that's the natural rhythm of how people read. So you don't want to attract the right to the middle of the page and then have to force them to go up against what the habitual used to to go up instead of down. Same for left to right. Normally I put my visuals on the left and the headline on the right because the visual will be seen first and the headline on the right. But that's not as firm a rule. We do notice elements that are out of place. So handwritten notes in the margin, usually very good. Sometimes if you say Mr. X reveals his secret, he works for the IRS and he's now telling you he's written a book on how to save on your taxes but we can't reveal his real name. If you have a picture of a Mr. X sitting in the middle of a page with his eyes blacked out, it adds a lot of intrigue because people notice little things that are out of place and you can use that to your advantage. Make your graphics suit your product's personality. It's very obvious. If you go into a high end market like investors, you should not make your graphics look like the National Enquirer. Which I guess rule was written after that test. Watch out for very wide columns. Rule of thumb I follow is don't make your columns wider than 40 to 60 characters long. Different designers like different widths but in that family it should be okay. You should make the eye go very long. Again, most people read the newspaper. There's short, narrow columns or even a book that don't go on forever and you don't want to read it to have to fight against that natural tendency to want to jump to the next line. Photos out pull illustrations. This has been tested many, many times because photos have more credibility. So if you're graphically portraying something, a copy machine or whatever, it's much better to use a real photo. Preferably a photo of the thing being used or turning out something that's a benefit that has some interesting invitation to see what's going on in this photo.

## [18:16 - 21:18]

Like Olga V's famous man in the half the way shirt. He used a distinguished looking gentleman modeling a half the way shirt and they weren't getting the camera angles ready just yet. And Olga V said it kind of looks kind of dull but then somebody had an eye patch and he said to put that eye patch on my model and he put the eye patch on the model and then the man in the half the way shirt now stood there with a beautiful shirt and an eye patch. It just gave him a swashbuckling look that drew much more attention to the ad. So look for those opportunities. Never ever let a photo or illustration interrupt your coupon rule. You'll be amazed even how many great direct marketers, very smart direct marketers do this. Here's a good example. This is a book about one of the great golfers of all time, Sammy Snead, who reveals all of his tips. The headline isn't all that great but there's a mortal sin being committed in the coupon. That's because any golfer who would love to respond to this ad has to cut Sammy's legs off. And they don't do it. Believe me that might seem it's not even a conscious decision. You'll have a very subconscious resistance to cutting any form of the human body. I saw this once for myself. I was taught that by a designer at Olga V. Mayer and we never did it but when we went to visit one of our clients they were giving us a tour of the building and we went through the mail room. They said, oh come see the responses because they didn't only test us, they would test other ad agencies as well and the other ad agency they were using had coupons with a little man standing in the coupon and his head and shoulders came above the coupon rule. And everybody who sent the coupon in took great pains to cut around his head. Nobody wanted to cut off the salesman's head and you can understand that it's a nerving thing to have to do. I mean it's funny but for every person who took the pain to go get a scissor to not have to cut off the person's head, there's probably somebody who heard the phone ring and that was a lost sale and that will really depress response if you make somebody have extra work and cutting out your coupon. And that's very common even from well-known direct marketing agencies. The designers can't resist it. They had so much to keep all the visuals and that tight little coupon rule or outside of it just seems to offend their sense of dynamism in the layout but it's a bad mistake. Start body copy with drop initials. I'm going to just run through a bunch of them. Big letter at the beginning of your copy. Most magazine articles do that. We said just before in body copy, serif type, out pulls, sans serif type. This is serif type. This is sans serif. In body copy avoid reverse type, wide on black. It depresses response. You'll see a lot of people making this mistake too. It'll be a black background filled with white type. It's begging for people not to read it. Long copy generally out pulls short copy but make it readable. You want to use a lot of good sub-heads. Break up the long pieces of copy. Keep your paragraphs to maybe six lines.

## [21:18 - 24:18]

I mean if you're going to really interrupt a thought you might want to let it go a little further but not too much. Keep an average of five or six lines. Sentences fairly short. Make it easy for somebody to read through your copy. This was one I learned recently. I never knew this. Never put a period after a headline. It depresses response. They actually did tests of this. Where a period at the end of a headline stops the reader from moving forward because a period means stop. It's again, it's an interruption between that thought and the one that you want to follow very quickly. Why put an unnecessary stop? Why put a red light between your headline and the rest of the ad? Likewise, never end page one of a sales letter with a period. It's a very critical moment when somebody is reading your letter as they get to the bottom of page one you want to have a sort of a cliffhanger at the very least have a sentence that is in mid-sentence so that it carries over. So it's a natural desire to finish the sentence and turn the page. If you end your last sentence on page one with a period, it's a full stop. Again, it's a perfect opportunity for your reader to stop reading and put the mailing aside and think they're going to get back to it tomorrow and sometimes that never happens. Photos should have captions. Readers look for them. You want to let your reader keep practicing his or her habits of reading. Whenever you see, especially an interesting photo, it's a wasted sales opportunity if you don't have a nice interesting caption under it because you've drawn the eye to that spot. They look under for a caption and it's a precious moment that you can use to sell something or reinforce the guarantee or whatever you want to do, but don't waste the opportunity to use a photo. Every photo should have a caption. Every letter, for the same reason, should end with at least one PS because studies have shown the PS is the second most highly read part of any letter after the headline. They've looked at readers of direct mail through two-way mirrors and they see how they open the package. Very often they'll open it up. They'll spread the things around and they'll look at the headline. They'll kind of flip to the back and if they see a PS, it's a great opportunity to reinforce the benefit. Very often they'll find that people will go to the order form first and those studies are surprising finding was when people open up the package, they look quickly around and they look at the order form because it's so much smarter than we get them credit for sometimes. Even our naive non-marketing executive clients, customers that we write to, they know the dealer summed up on the order form. That's how smart they are. So they write to the order form. What's this about? What's it going to cost? So you must have your order form. It should always be a miniature ad. These include the benefit because that might be your only shot at convincing them to read more. Don't kill widows. They go the breath of life. Now these are not the widows that were selling insurance. The widows are the little bits of type at the end of a paragraph. Like a one-word falls on its own line. Well, here we have of life would be a widow at the end of a long paragraph. And lots of editors love to scratch those. It's like not good form. But one of the great designers and copyrights of all time, Fairfax Cone said, no, don't

## [24:18 - 27:19]

kill widows. They air out the layout. They're sort of a breath of life. And actually they've done studies of that where there are more widows. The ad is easier to read. So I just don't worry about them either way. I'm not going to try to create them or get rid of them if the sentence is written well. I don't want to artificially cut it down by just to avoid a widow. You subheads as recharging stations to boost flagging interest. All the skill that you put into writing a headline should also be used to write subheads, which are almost as important as your headlines to keep flagging interest from resulting in just a decision. I don't want to read this anymore. The subheads, and you should have one on every page of a long letter, should always be used to re-boost the reader's interest. Mention this one before. Keep paragraphs no longer than five to seven lines. Some products demand dominant visuals. Sometimes your product is a conceptual one, and usually you'll want to make your headline the main thought. But if you're selling a beautiful artwork or jewelry or collectible plates or crystal glass or in our case when we were showing tips for it to be more beautiful and stay younger, you want to show somebody who exemplifies those principles. So always ask, is this more of a copy-driven sale? Or is there maybe a better question is, how can we bring visuals into this that really reinforce the selling message? And for some products, when you ask that question, they should actually dominate. Most direct mail packages, the letter, traditionally has always been the heart of the mailing. And even in these magilogues and bookilogues and other formats where there is no loose letter, I always put that right up front as the welcoming message from the editor or from whomever is inviting the person to look through this wonderful magazine or book to find the secrets. Just explain the proposition and welcome the person to the piece. And that way you get the best of both. You get the personal feeling of a letter inside your magazine, which is not a terribly personal type of piece. In direct mail, a letter sometimes is actually, pulls a lower response when you include a brochure. It seems counterintuitive. Very often a copy chief or marketing director will say, I need a letter and a brochure for such a size of product. Well, you might want to test that combination because sometimes the brochure depresses response. You could never send. I've never heard of a successful piece. I'm sure there is one somewhere, but sending a brochure without a letter, I don't think could ever work. I've never heard of it working for any of the people I've worked with. But a letter very often will work better without a brochure. So always question if the brochure is strictly necessary. And the reason why is because, again, it flags itself as sales message. Personal letter is just that. It's very personal. It's warm. It's friendly. It opens that envelope and sees a colorful brochure and a letter because the brochure is so colorful, it will attract the eye first to exactly the wrong piece of copy.

## [27:19 - 30:20]

That will be more like product-driven copy, not the friendly introduction of your headline. So you're misdirecting them to the wrong message first by having a brochure in the package. But I would test it. Some products need more explanation. So you might need a brochure, but be very careful with it. Make sure your letter emerges from the envelope face forward and headline above the fold. I hear people laughing relating to that message because how often do you spend so much time crafting an important message and the printer inserts it so that what you see is like the back of the letter and it just comes out. It's like a blank panel. As soon as the person opens the, I don't have a sample here, but opens the front of the letter, his face up, and then the person usually turns it over to open up the flap. And the first thing out should be the headline of your letter above the fold. It shouldn't be a brochure or anything else that gets between the reader and the first impression of the headline on the letter. But that's such a bugaboo, which may be because you work so hard to make that message come out and they're exactly the right place at exactly the right moment, and then the production people just put it in backwards and the person has to fumble around to get to the beginning of the letter. This is a great one. Make sure your response device fits inside your return envelope. Do you ever have to respond with a little envelope and a giant order form and you'll fold it up and you put it in, you lick the envelope and then the glue isn't strong enough to hold it, it'll pop up and you say, the heck with the whole thing, I didn't want it that much anyway. Make sure that the reply device fits inside your envelope. Very simple things, but from experience, these are some of the things that have gone wrong with my mailings. Make sure your address and contact information are on every piece in the package. So often people will throw away every piece but one, and I'll say, I'm going to order this, but then they look at it, oh my goodness, there's no phone number, there's no place to order, nothing, it's just my favorite part of the mailing I put over here and now I don't know where to order and they'll throw it away. So every single piece in a direct mail package should have the information of where to contact you. Always look at your advertise in the same way your prospects will. Now this was a very interesting thing that Olga V taught us when I was working in his agency and he said, look, here's what happens at a typical Ed agency. The men and women who are creating the campaign, it's a lot of fun. You brainstorm and each person does what they're supposed to do. The artist does his hard job and the copyrighter does theirs and you bring it together and then you run over to the client. Everybody's waiting breathlessly if the client will like it and what normally happens is these clients, they have you come in and there's a big boardroom, big table and you've got to make things big enough so that the person sitting at that end of the table will read it or else they might not like that ad. So if you ever wonder how ads get written that look like this, which is a mistake Olga V said nobody in his agency should ever make, where an ad comes out like almost totally unreadable, then it's like this was written, I'm sure, to let everybody in the room, from any corner of the room, see the headline. And when the client approves it, it says, oh, that sounds good.

## [30:20 - 30:58]

Everybody runs back and goes with that. But in effect what they did was they created an ad that's like a billboard. Somebody reading a newspaper like this has almost stepped back to read it, typed that in large. They wrote it for the people in the boardroom, not for the person on the train reading the newspaper. But because the client approved it, oh, we can't change it now, let's run with it and we'll go, this is how accidents like that happen. And if you open any newspaper in your room, wherever you're staying today and see Wall Street Journal and New York Times, you'll see ads that make this mistake. This happens all the time. They go to the client, show it large enough so that a person in the boardroom 10 feet away can read it and that's what they run with in the newspapers. So that's the end of what little I know about design.
